items.subtopic: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites | ABDOMINAL SWELLING | CAUSES
items.title: Understanding the Six Fs of Abdominal Swelling
items.content: The causes of abdominal swelling can be categorized into six main groups: flatus, fat, fluid, fetus, feces, or a fatal growth. Each category represents different underlying conditions that lead to increased abdominal girth.
keyPoints: Flatus involves increased intestinal gas with ~200 mL normally present in the small intestine.
Fat accumulation can be due to lifestyle factors or diseases like Cushing's syndrome.
Fluid, specifically ascites, is categorized into three grades: Grade 1 (detectable by ultrasonography), Grade 2 (detectable by physical examination), and Grade 3 (marked abdominal distention).
Fetus-related swelling typically begins at 12–14 weeks of gestation.
Feces accumulation due to constipation or obstruction can increase abdominal girth.
Fatal growth includes neoplasms, abscesses, cysts, organ enlargement, and aneurysms causing distention.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · Abdominal Swelling and Ascites | ABDOMINAL SWELLING | CAUSES

items.subtopic: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites | ABDOMINAL SWELLING | CAUSES
items.title: Diagnosing Flatus-Related Abdominal Distension
items.content: Increased intestinal gas, or flatus, can cause abdominal swelling. This may result from aerophagia, celiac disease, gastroparesis, or bacterial overgrowth leading to fermentation of FODMAPs.
keyPoints: Aerophagia involves swallowing air due to gulping food, chewing gum, smoking, or anxiety.
Celiac disease and gastroparesis are associated with bloating and distention.
Bacterial metabolism of FODMAPs can produce hydrogen, carbon dioxide, or methane.
Impaired intestinal transit or visceral hypersensitivity may contribute to the subjective sense of abdominal pressure.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · Abdominal Swelling and Ascites | ABDOMINAL SWELLING | CAUSES

items.subtopic: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites | ABDOMINAL SWELLING | CAUSES
items.title: Recognizing Fluid Accumulation in Ascites
items.content: Ascites is a significant cause of abdominal swelling due to fluid accumulation. It is graded based on detectability: Grade 1 by ultrasonography, Grade 2 by physical examination, and Grade 3 with marked distention.
keyPoints: Grade 1 ascites is only detectable via ultrasonography.
Grade 2 ascites can be detected through physical examination.
Grade 3 ascites results in noticeable abdominal distention.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · Abdominal Swelling and Ascites | ABDOMINAL SWELLING | CAUSES

items.title: Identifying Etiology of Abdominal Swelling through History and Physical Exam
items.content: A thorough history-taking is crucial for determining the etiology of abdominal swelling. Key elements include onset, timing, relationship to food or bowel movements, prior surgery, dietary habits, medications, and symptoms suggestive of malignancy such as weight loss, night sweats, and anorexia. Bowel obstruction signs include inability to pass stool or flatus with nausea or vomiting. Ascites should be considered in patients with risk factors for chronic liver disease like excessive alcohol use and jaundice.
keyPoints: Ask about onset and timing of bloating/distension
Inquire about symptoms suggestive of malignancy: weight loss, night sweats, anorexia
Assess for bowel obstruction signs: inability to pass stool/flatus with nausea/vomiting
Consider ascites in patients with chronic liver disease risk factors: excessive alcohol use, jaundice
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · HISTORY | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

items.title: Physical Examination for Abdominal Swelling and Ascites
items.content: The physical exam should assess systemic signs of disease. Lymphadenopathy, especially supraclavicular (Virchow's node), suggests metastatic abdominal malignancy. Cardiac examination includes evaluating JVP elevation, Kussmaul's sign, pericardial knock, and tricuspid regurgitation murmur. Signs like spider angiomas, palmar erythema, caput medusae, and gynecomastia indicate liver disease. Abdominal exam involves inspection for distention/mass, auscultation for bowel sounds or bruits, percussion to differentiate gas from fluid/mass, and palpation for tenderness, mass, organ enlargement, or nodularity.
keyPoints: Inspect for uneven distention or mass
Auscultate for absence of bowel sounds or high-pitched localized sounds (intestinal obstruction/ileus)
Percuss: tympanic sound suggests gas; dullness suggests fluid/mass
Palpate for tenderness, mass, organ enlargement, nodularity
Minimum 1500 mL of ascitic fluid required for detection on physical exam
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · HISTORY | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

items.title: Imaging Modalities for Ascites Detection
items.content: Abdominal ultrasonography is a sensitive tool for detecting ascitic fluid, capable of identifying as little as 100 mL. It also assesses hepatosplenomegaly and liver nodularity but may be inadequate for retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy or pancreatic lesions due to bowel gas interference.
keyPoints: Ultrasonography can detect as little as 100 mL of ascitic fluid.
CT is preferred if malignancy, pancreatic disease, or advanced cirrhosis and portal hypertension are suspected.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · IMAGING AND LABORATORY EVALUATION | ASCITES

items.title: Laboratory Evaluation for Ascites Pathogenesis
items.content: Assessment of hepatic function in ascites includes liver biochemical tests, serum albumin, prothrombin time (INR), and complete blood count. These help evaluate portal hypertension effects and systemic infections. Serum amylase and lipase are checked for acute pancreatitis, while urinary protein quantitation is used when nephrotic syndrome is suspected.
keyPoints: Laboratory evaluation includes liver biochemical tests, serum albumin, prothrombin time (INR), and complete blood count.
Serum amylase and lipase are checked for acute pancreatitis; urinary protein quantitation for nephrotic syndrome.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · IMAGING AND LABORATORY EVALUATION | ASCITES

items.subtopic: PATHOGENESIS IN THE ABSENCE OF CIRRHOSIS | CAUSES
items.title: Peritoneal Carcinomatosis as a Cause of Ascites in Non-Cirrhotic Patients
items.content: Ascites can develop due to peritoneal carcinomatosis, where tumor cells line the peritoneum and produce protein-rich fluid. This mechanism is similar to tuberculous peritonitis, which involves tubercles exuding a proteinaceous fluid.
keyPoints: Peritoneal carcinomatosis results from primary or metastatic malignancies like mesothelioma, sarcoma, gastric adenocarcinoma, colonic adenocarcinoma, breast carcinoma, lung carcinoma, and melanoma.
Tumor cells produce protein-rich fluid contributing to ascites development.
Similar mechanism in tuberculous peritonitis with proteinaceous fluid from tubercles.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · PATHOGENESIS IN THE ABSENCE OF CIRRHOSIS | CAUSES

items.subtopic: PATHOGENESIS IN THE ABSENCE OF CIRRHOSIS | CAUSES
items.title: Pancreatic Ascites and Its Pathogenesis
items.content: Ascites can occur due to pancreatic ascites, where leakage of pancreatic enzymes into the peritoneum leads to fluid accumulation.
keyPoints: Pancreatic ascites results from leakage of pancreatic enzymes.
Marked hypoalbuminemia as a cause includes nephrotic syndrome, protein-losing enteropathy, or malnutrition.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · PATHOGENESIS IN THE ABSENCE OF CIRRHOSIS | CAUSES

items.title: Paracentesis: A Key Procedure in Ascites Evaluation
items.content: Paracentesis is a bedside procedure used to extract ascitic fluid for determining the etiology of ascites. It involves inserting a needle or catheter into the peritoneum, typically in the left lower quadrant due to its advantages.
keyPoints: Preferred site: Left lower quadrant due to greater depth and thinner abdominal wall.
Safety: Safe even with coagulopathy; complications are infrequent.

Indications for gross appearance examination: Turbid fluid suggests infection or tumor cells, white milky fluid indicates chylous ascites (triglyceride level >200 mg/dL), dark brown fluid suggests biliary tract perforation, and black fluid may indicate pancreatic necrosis or metastatic melanoma.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · EVALUATION

items.title: Serum-Ascites Albumin Gradient (SAAG) in Ascites Diagnosis
items.content: The SAAG is calculated by subtracting the ascitic albumin concentration from the serum albumin level. It helps distinguish between portal hypertensive and nonportal hypertensive causes of ascites.
keyPoints: Calculation: Serum albumin - Ascitic albumin.
SAAG ≥ 1.1 g/dL indicates portal hypertension (e.g., cirrhosis, cardiac ascites).
SAAG < 1.1 g/dL suggests nonportal hypertensive causes (e.g., tuberculous peritonitis, nephrotic syndrome).
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · EVALUATION

items.title: Further Diagnostic Tests for Specific Ascites Etiologies
items.content: Additional tests are performed based on clinical suspicion. For high-SAAG ascites, protein levels and BNP can provide further etiological clues.
keyPoints: Ascitic protein level ≥ 2.5 g/dL suggests normal hepatic sinusoids (e.g., cardiac ascites).
Ascitic protein level < 2.5 g/dL indicates damaged hepatic sinusoids (e.g., cirrhosis).
High BNP levels in serum suggest heart failure as a cause of high-SAAG ascites.
Secondary peritonitis: Ascitic glucose <50 mg/dL, ascitic LDH > serum LDH, multiple pathogens on culture.
Pancreatic ascites: Ascitic amylase level typically >1000 mg/dL.
Tuberculous peritonitis: Elevated ascitic adenosine deaminase (>90% sensitivity with a cut-off of 30–45 U/L).
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · EVALUATION

items.title: Initial Management and Diuretic Therapy for Cirrhotic Ascites
items.content: The initial treatment for cirrhotic ascites involves a moderate restriction of sodium intake to 2 g/d. If this alone is insufficient, oral diuretics such as spironolactone (an aldosterone antagonist) combined with furosemide are used. Spironolactone's use may be limited by side effects like hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and painful gynecomastia; amiloride can substitute for spironolactone if needed. Furosemide is typically combined in a 40:100 ratio with spironolactone, with maximum daily doses of 400 mg and 160 mg respectively.
keyPoints: Sodium intake restriction to 2 g/d
Diuretic combination: Spironolactone (aldosterone antagonist) + Furosemide (loop diuretic)
Spironolactone side effects: Hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, painful gynecomastia
Amiloride as a substitute for spironolactone if needed
Furosemide to Spironolactone ratio of 40:100
Maximal daily doses: Spironolactone 400 mg, Furosemide 160 mg
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · Ascites

items.title: Management Strategies for Refractory Cirrhotic Ascites
items.content: Refractory cirrhotic ascites is defined by persistence of ascites despite sodium restriction and maximum diuretic use or side effects that preclude maximal doses. Management includes adding midodrine or clonidine to improve systemic hemodynamics. When medical therapy fails, options include large-volume paracentesis (LVP) with IV albumin infusion (6–8 g/L of fluid removed) or transjugular intrahepatic peritoneal shunt (TIPS). TIPS reduces ascites reaccumulation but increases hepatic encephalopathy risk. The Alfapump system is another option, though it has technical and renal dysfunction issues.
keyPoints: Refractory cirrhotic ascites: Persistence despite sodium restriction and maximum diuretic use
Add midodrine or clonidine to improve hemodynamics
Large-volume paracentesis (LVP) with IV albumin infusion (6–8 g/L of fluid removed)
TIPS reduces reaccumulation but increases hepatic encephalopathy risk
Alfapump system: High technical difficulties and renal dysfunction
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · Ascites

items.title: Diagnosing Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis (SBP) in Cirrhotic Ascites
items.content: SBP is a frequent and serious complication of cirrhotic ascites, characterized by an elevated polymorphonuclear neutrophil (PMN) count in the ascitic fluid. It can present with symptoms such as increased abdominal girth, fever, nausea, vomiting, or worsening hepatic encephalopathy.
keyPoints: SBP is defined by a PMN count of ≥250/μL in ascitic fluid.
Symptoms include increased abdominal girth (common), abdominal tenderness (40% of patients), and fever.
Paracentesis within 12 hours of admission reduces mortality due to early detection.
Common pathogens: gram-negative rods (e.g., E. coli, Klebsiella), streptococci, enterococci.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · COMPLICATIONS

items.title: Management and Treatment of Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis (SBP)
items.content: Treatment for SBP typically involves a third-generation cephalosporin like IV cefotaxime, administered at 2 g every 12 hours. A 5-day course is usually sufficient if the patient shows clinical improvement. Monitoring includes repeat paracentesis after 48 hours to ensure a decrease in ascitic PMN count by at least 25%.
keyPoints: Initial treatment: IV cefotaxime, 2 g every 12 hours for 5 days.
Repeat paracentesis recommended after 48 hours of therapy.
Confirm reduction in ascitic PMN count by ≥25% from baseline.
Be aware of multidrug-resistant bacteria in nosocomial or healthcare-acquired SBP.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · COMPLICATIONS

items.explanation: The six Fs are flatus, fat, fluid, fetus, feces, and fatal growth; fever is not included.

items.question: What condition can result from the swallowing of air leading to increased abdominal girth?
items.explanation: Aerophagia, or the swallowing of air, can lead to increased amounts of oxygen and nitrogen in the small intestine causing abdominal swelling.

items.explanation: Pregnancy, or the presence of a fetus, leads to an increase in abdominal size.

items.question: Which symptom combination is most suggestive of bowel obstruction?
options: Inability to pass stool or flatus with nausea or vomiting
Weight loss, night sweats, and anorexia
items.explanation: Bowel obstruction is suggested by the inability to pass stool or flatus together with nausea or vomiting.

items.question: Which physical examination finding suggests metastatic abdominal malignancy?
items.explanation: The presence of supraclavicular lymphadenopathy, or Virchow's node, suggests metastatic abdominal malignancy.

items.question: Which laboratory test is used to assess hepatic function in patients with suspected cirrhosis?
options: Liver biochemical testing, serum albumin level measurement, and prothrombin time determination
items.explanation: Liver biochemical testing, serum albumin level measurement, and prothrombin time are used to assess hepatic function.

items.question: Which condition can be diagnosed using tests that detect increased amounts of hydrogen and methane in expired breath?
items.explanation: Increased hydrogen and methane in expired air are used to diagnose carbohydrate malabsorption, such as lactose.

items.question: Which condition is NOT a common cause of ascites in the absence of cirrhosis?
items.explanation: Hypothyroidism is a rare cause of ascites, unlike the other options.

items.subtopic: PATHOGENESIS IN THE ABSENCE OF CIRRHOSIS | CAUSES
items.question: What mechanism contributes to ascites in peritoneal carcinomatosis?
options: Tumor cells produce a protein-poor fluid.
Fluid is drawn from the extracellular space into the peritoneum.
Ascites results from leakage of pancreatic enzymes.
items.explanation: Tumor cells produce a protein-rich fluid, and fluid from the extracellular space is drawn into the peritoneum.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · PATHOGENESIS IN THE ABSENCE OF CIRRHOSIS | CAUSES

items.subtopic: PATHOGENESIS IN THE ABSENCE OF CIRRHOSIS | CAUSES
items.question: Which of the following conditions can cause ascites due to marked hypoalbuminemia?
items.explanation: Marked hypoalbuminemia may result from nephrotic syndrome, protein-losing enteropathy, or malnutrition.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · PATHOGENESIS IN THE ABSENCE OF CIRRHOSIS | CAUSES

items.question: Which site is preferred for paracentesis due to greater depth of ascites and thinner abdominal wall?
items.explanation: The left lower quadrant is preferred for paracentesis because of the greater depth of ascites and the thinner abdominal wall.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · EVALUATION

items.question: What does a serum-ascites albumin gradient (SAAG) $≥1.1$ g/dL indicate?
items.explanation: A SAAG $≥1.1$ g/dL reflects the presence of portal hypertension.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · EVALUATION

items.question: What does a high ascitic protein level ($≥2.5$ g/dL) in high-SAAG ascites suggest?
items.explanation: An ascitic protein level $≥2.5$ g/dL indicates that the hepatic sinusoids are normal and allowing passage of protein into the ascites.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · EVALUATION

items.question: Which condition is indicated by an ascitic fluid amylase level typically >1000 mg/dL?
items.explanation: When pancreatic ascites is suspected, the ascitic amylase level should be measured and is typically >1000 mg/dL.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · EVALUATION

items.question: What is the initial treatment for cirrhotic ascites?
options: Moderate restriction of sodium intake to 2 g/d
items.explanation: The initial treatment for cirrhotic ascites is moderate restriction of sodium intake to 2 g/d.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · Ascites

items.question: Which combination of diuretics is typically used when sodium restriction alone does not control ascites?
items.explanation: The combination of spironolactone and furosemide is typically used to increase urinary sodium excretion when sodium restriction alone is inadequate.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · Ascites

items.question: What is the recommended IV albumin infusion rate during large-volume paracentesis for cirrhotic ascites?
items.explanation: Patients undergoing large-volume paracentesis should receive IV albumin infusions of 6–8 g/L of ascitic fluid removed.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · Ascites

items.question: Which of the following conditions is least likely to be complicated by spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP)?
items.explanation: SBP is rare in malignant ascites.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · COMPLICATIONS

items.question: What is the diagnostic criterion for spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) based on ascitic fluid analysis?
items.explanation: SBP is defined by a polymorphonuclear neutrophil (PMN) count of ≥250/μL in the ascitic fluid.

items.question: Which antibiotic regimen has traditionally been used for treating spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP)?
options: Oral metronidazole, 500 mg three times daily
items.explanation: Treatment of SBP has traditionally been with a third-generation cephalosporin such as IV cefotaxime, 2 g every 12 hours.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · COMPLICATIONS

items.statement: Increased intestinal gas can result from bacterial metabolism of excess fermentable substances.
items.explanation: Bacterial metabolism of substances like lactose, fructose, and other FODMAPs can lead to increased production of gases such as hydrogen, carbon dioxide, or methane.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · Abdominal Swelling and Ascites | ABDOMINAL SWELLING | CAUSES

items.subtopic: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites | ABDOMINAL SWELLING | CAUSES
items.statement: Ascites is always detectable by physical examination.
items.explanation: Grade 1 ascites is only detectable by ultrasonography, not by physical examination.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · Abdominal Swelling and Ascites | ABDOMINAL SWELLING | CAUSES

items.statement: An abdomen filled with gas is dull to percussion.
items.explanation: An abdomen filled with gas is tympanic, not dull, on percussion.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · HISTORY | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

items.statement: Spider angiomas and palmar erythema are signs of liver disease.
items.explanation: Spider angiomas and palmar erythema suggest liver disease.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · HISTORY | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

items.statement: CT scans can be used to detect retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy effectively.
items.explanation: CT is often inadequate for detecting retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy due to overlying bowel gas.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · IMAGING AND LABORATORY EVALUATION | ASCITES

items.statement: Increased intrahepatic vasoconstriction in cirrhosis leads to decreased nitric oxide production.
items.explanation: Cirrhosis is associated with a decrease in endothelial nitric oxide synthetase (eNOS) production, resulting in decreased nitric oxide and increased intrahepatic vasoconstriction.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · IMAGING AND LABORATORY EVALUATION | ASCITES

items.subtopic: PATHOGENESIS IN THE ABSENCE OF CIRRHOSIS | CAUSES
items.statement: Peritoneal carcinomatosis can result from primary peritoneal malignancies such as mesothelioma.
items.explanation: Mesothelioma is a primary peritoneal malignancy that can cause peritoneal carcinomatosis.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · PATHOGENESIS IN THE ABSENCE OF CIRRHOSIS | CAUSES

items.subtopic: PATHOGENESIS IN THE ABSENCE OF CIRRHOSIS | CAUSES
items.statement: Tuberculous peritonitis causes ascites by producing a protein-poor fluid.
items.explanation: Tuberculous peritonitis exudes a protein-aceous fluid, not a protein-poor one.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · PATHOGENESIS IN THE ABSENCE OF CIRRHOSIS | CAUSES

items.statement: A SAAG <1.1 g/dL indicates that the ascites is related to portal hypertension.
items.explanation: A SAAG <1.1 g/dL indicates that the ascites is not related to portal hypertension.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · EVALUATION

items.statement: Secondary peritonitis can be suggested by an ascitic glucose level <50 mg/dL.
items.explanation: Secondary peritonitis is suggested by an ascitic glucose level <50 mg/dL.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · EVALUATION

items.statement: Beta blockers are recommended for patients with refractory cirrhotic ascites to improve survival rates.
items.explanation: The use of beta blockers in patients with refractory ascites may be associated with decreased survival rates.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · Ascites

items.statement: Tuberculous peritonitis causing ascites is treated with standard antituberculosis therapy.
items.explanation: Ascites caused by tuberculous peritonitis is indeed treated with standard antituberculosis therapy.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · Ascites

items.statement: Rebound tenderness is common in patients presenting with spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP).
items.explanation: Rebound tenderness is uncommon in SBP.

items.statement: Repeat paracentesis after 48 hours of antibiotic therapy for SBP should show a decrease in the ascitic PMN count by at least 25% from baseline.

items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · COMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites | ABDOMINAL SWELLING | CAUSES
items.assertion: Increased abdominal girth can result from an imbalance between caloric intake and energy expenditure.
items.reason: This imbalance leads to weight gain, particularly in the form of increased abdominal fat.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · Abdominal Swelling and Ascites | ABDOMINAL SWELLING | CAUSES

items.subtopic: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites | ABDOMINAL SWELLING | CAUSES
items.assertion: A neoplasm can cause abdominal swelling.
items.reason: Neoplasms often lead to increased production of intestinal gas.
items.explanation: While a neoplasm can cause abdominal swelling, it does not do so by increasing intestinal gas production.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · Abdominal Swelling and Ascites | ABDOMINAL SWELLING | CAUSES

items.assertion: A SAAG $≥1.1$ g/dL indicates portal hypertension.
items.reason: The SAAG reflects the pressure within the hepatic sinusoids and correlates with the hepatic venous pressure gradient.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
items.reference: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites · EVALUATION

items.reason: A low SAAG indicates that ascites is not related to portal hypertension.
items.explanation: A is false, but R is true; a SAAG <1.1 g/dL does not indicate cirrhosis.
